does: This paper introduces the Causal Sensitivity Score (CSS), an interventional evaluation metric that assesses whether clinical language models and agents appropriately update their medical recommendations when key case features change. The authors systematically mutate 224 oncology cases along five clinically meaningful dimensions—biomarker flips, treatment failures, biomarker removals, surgery-status changes, and stage perturbations—and measure whether models adjust recommendations in the correct direction using a {0, 0.5, 1.0} grading scale. The core finding reveals a critical divergence: six frontier models that rank nearly identically on traditional coverage-based metrics (Consensus Match Score) exhibit dramatically different performance on CSS, with the worst CMS model becoming the best on CSS and rank orders nearly completely reversed. The analysis also uncovers a universal safety failure across all evaluated models in surgery-status perturbations.
connects: This work extends the growing recognition that static benchmarks inadequately capture language model capability, building on recent critiques of coverage-based evaluation metrics in clinical domains. It responds to limitations of existing clinical AI evaluation frameworks like the Consensus Match Score, which measure recall against reference responses but fail to capture causal reasoning or robustness to input perturbations. The paper draws on causal inference methodology and interventional approaches from machine learning, applying counterfactual analysis principles to clinical decision-making. It connects to broader work on probing hidden capabilities and failure modes in large language models, and aligns with emerging interest in understanding how models behave under distribution shift and input modification rather than just aggregate accuracy metrics.
enables: This framework opens paths toward more rigorous evaluation of clinical AI systems where causal reasoning fidelity directly impacts patient safety. The CSS methodology could extend to other high-stakes domains requiring robust decision-making under information change—radiology, cardiology, and surgical planning. The paper establishes a template for pre-registered interventional metrics that could become standard practice in clinical AI evaluation, moving the field away from benchmark gaming toward genuine capability assessment. The identified universal failure on surgery-status changes points toward specific targeted improvements in model training and prompting. More broadly, this work enables developers to surface and remediate hidden capability gaps before deployment, while enabling regulators and clinicians to identify which systems genuinely understand clinical reasoning versus those that superficially match reference answers.
